coredata.dc:title: Exploring barley bran as a precursor for carbon quantum dots with enhanced fluorescence characteristics

coredata.dc:description: The research described in this article deals with the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from barley bran including husk (BB) enriched with nitrogen by a hydrothermal-microwave method. Barley bran, as a raw material, is distinguished by its high carbon content and the presence of chemical compounds such as polysaccharides, proteins, fatty acids, and triglycerides, which contribute to the significant presence of oxygen atoms. The BB material was characterized using techniques such as Roentgen diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), dry weight analysis, scanning electron microscopy with energy dispersive spectroscopy (SEM-EDS), and CHNOS elemental analysis. The results of XRD and FT-IR analysis were compared with those for dried CQDs, which show significant structural changes. Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) was used to analyze the size and structure of CQDs. The particles with a crystalline structure were characterized by an average size of 5.5 nm and a distance between crystal planes in the range of 0.19–0.28 nm. The optical properties of the CQDs particles were checked by UV–Vis spectroscopy analysis and their ability to emit light by spectrofluorimetry. The suspensions were characterized by absorption at 280 nm and fluorescence at 380 nm excitation with an emission peak base of about 200 nm and a peak maximum of 464 nm. The analysis showed that process parameters such as temperature, synthesis time, and filtrate volume significantly affect the properties of the obtained CQDs. The best fluorescence results were obtained for samples synthesized at 235 °C or 250 °C with larger filtrate volumes. Analysis of the optimization of the synthesis process showed that the best optical properties were obtained for samples prepared at 250 °C for 1 h from a filtrate volume of 40 mL. The study also underscores the need to further improve product purification procedures and reduce particle aggregation in aqueous solutions, which is an important element in improving the stability and performance of CQDs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Composition of different fractions of barley grain and graphical representation of grain structure of cereals (own elaboration based on articles from references [7–18]).
Schematic representation of the extraction, synthesis, and purification procedures of nitrogen-doped CQDs from barley bran raw material.
Comparison of the results of the analysis of raw BB material before thermal treatment and sample C235-3-25: (A) - XRD diffractogram, (B) - FT-IR spectrum with the most important bands highlighted.
Analysis surface structure for the BB raw material before thermal treatment (A-C) with SEM microphotograph with EDS elemental analysis results (D).
The UV–Vis spectrum of the obtained CQDs suspensions with 100-fold dilution.

Results of CQD size analysis in sample C
235-3-25 : (A, B) - TEM microphotographs showing the structures analyzed, including (B) close-ups on the crystal planes of the particles; (C) - histogram of the particle size distribution based on the microphotographs, along with the fitted normal distribution; and (D) - analysis of the particle size distribution by DLS.

The fluorescence emission spectra (A) - for CQDs samples and QS fluorescence standard solution (conc. 10(−5) M) at 347 nm excitation, (B) - for CQDs samples at 380 nm excitation with peak maximum marked at 464 nm, and (C) - photos of the water reference sample and the C250-5-40 sample in quartz cuvettes in daylight and the darkroom after exposure to a UV lamp with λ = 360 nm.

Profile of predicted values and desirability index for the synthesis of CQDs under different process conditions: analysis of the effect of process temperature, process time and filtrate volume on peak absorbance at 280 nm, and maximum fluorescence emission at 380 nm excitation.
Independent parameters used in the conducted research plan in the synthesis of CQDs from the filtrate of BB raw material.
Summary of accurate results for fluorescence emission peaks of samples when excited with 380 nm light.
Comparison of fluorescence properties of biomass-derived CQDs synthesized by various methods, with focus on their diagnostic and imaging applications.

head.ce:title: Exploring barley bran as a precursor for carbon quantum dots with enhanced fluorescence characteristics

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The research described in this article deals with the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from barley bran including husk (BB) enriched with nitrogen by a hydrothermal-microwave method. Barley bran, as a raw material, is distinguished by its high carbon content and the presence of chemical compounds such as polysaccharides, proteins, fatty acids, and triglycerides, which contribute to the significant presence of oxygen atoms. The BB material was characterized using techniques such as Roentgen diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), dry weight analysis, scanning electron microscopy with energy dispersive spectroscopy (SEM-EDS), and CHNOS elemental analysis. The results of XRD and FT-IR analysis were compared with those for dried CQDs, which show significant structural changes. Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) was used to analyze the size and structure of CQDs. The particles with a crystalline structure were characterized by an average size of 5.5 nm and a distance between crystal planes in the range of 0.19–0.28 nm. The optical properties of the CQDs particles were checked by UV–Vis spectroscopy analysis and their ability to emit light by spectrofluorimetry. The suspensions were characterized by absorption at 280 nm and fluorescence at 380 nm excitation with an emission peak base of about 200 nm and a peak maximum of 464 nm. The analysis showed that process parameters such as temperature, synthesis time, and filtrate volume significantly affect the properties of the obtained CQDs. The best fluorescence results were obtained for samples synthesized at 235 °C or 250 °C with larger filtrate volumes. Analysis of the optimization of the synthesis process showed that the best optical properties were obtained for samples prepared at 250 °C for 1 h from a filtrate volume of 40 mL. The study also underscores the need to further improve product purification procedures and reduce particle aggregation in aqueous solutions, which is an important element in improving the stability and performance of CQDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The processing of grain raw materials generates by-products and waste. As a result of threshing, milling and sifting barley grains into flour, bran with valuable nutritional values is separated. They are used as additives and supplements to a balanced diet [1]. Barley bran is consumed with dairy products (milk, kefir, yogurt) or used as a thickener in baked goods, soups, and sauces. Most commonly, the bran, as well as barley itself, is used as animal feed, to make malt for beer and other alcoholic beverages [2,3,4].

In 2022, the world will produce 154.88 million tons of barley, which translates into a gross production value of 45.27 billion $ [5]. Most barley is grown in countries belonging to the European Union [1]. In the 2023/24 season, the amount of barley produced worldwide is similar to previous years, at 142.87 million tons. It ranks fourth in terms of global grain production. It is overtaken by corn production, which is larger by about 8.6 times, wheat by about 5.5 times, and rice by about 3.6 times [6]. Mostly barley is grown in areas where conditions are not suitable for the growth of corn or rice, which is in greater demand [7].

Cereal grains consist of four main components: the embryo, endosperm, fruit-seed coat, and hull. The embryo accounts for only about 3.0 % of grain weight and contains compounds from the protein, lipid and tocopherol groups. The endosperm, rich in starch and protein, accounts for about 77.2 % of the weight of the grain. It is the most nutritious part of the grain due to its role as a storehouse of nutrients for the embryo. Above all, it is the endosperm that is processed into flour [7–10]. The embryo and endosperm are surrounded by an inner layer composed of aleurone. It accounts for about 5.5 % of the weight of the grain and consists of lipids, proteins, minerals, B vitamins (niacin, folate), and most importantly, β-glucan, and arabinoxylan. The fruit-seed cover, in which the so-called pericarp and testa are distinguished, is about 3.3 % of the grain weight. These coverings are more commonly known as bran. Depending on how the grain is milled, the bran may also contain part of the aleurone layer and the outer covering of the grain. The last layer is the hull, which is a source of cellulose, lignin and even silicon. It covers the grain and can make up to 13.0 % of its weight [3,11–13].

Barley bran contains a high amount of fiber and more minerals and fat compared to endosperm [14]. Depending on the cereal variety, there may also be more β-glucan (3.7–15.4 %) and arabinoxylan (8.0–10.3 %) in the casing than in the flour obtained from endosperm [12]. Bran can also be high in starch at 27.8–57.1 % of the total. They also contain a high content of proteins (14.3–19.3 %), which primarily include albumin and globulins [15]. Among the amino acids per crude protein, glutamic acid (20.6–26.6 g/100 g), proline (9.9–11.9 g/100 g), aspartic acid (6.4–8.6 g/100 g) and leucine (6.6–7.5 g/100 g) are most abundant in the bran [15]. The crude oil extracted from barley bran yields the most linoleic acid and palmitic acid, which account for about 75.1 % and 20.6 % of all fatty acids in the crude, respectively. Glycolipids and phospholipids account for 4.2 % and 1.3 % of all lipids [16]. Bran is rich in vitamin E and other compounds of the tocopherol group. They also have more minerals than the rest of the grain. Iron content in bran is the highest at 128 μg/g. Zinc, phosphorus and sulfur are also marked, but in smaller amounts than in other parts of the grain [11,15]. Bran is distinguished by its high content of flavonoids, which translates into its antioxidant properties. Depending on the variety of barley used, these range from 48.5 μg/g to as high as 124.8 μg/g [17]. Phenolic acids such as caffeic acid, p-coumaric acid, ferulic acid, sinapic acid and syringic acid are detected in extracts obtained from barley bran [11]. The purple highland barley variety, on the other hand, is rich in anthocyanins [18].

Barley and its bran, due to their rich composition, are a valuable raw material with a wide range of applications, not only in the food industry. They can be used both to extract functional compounds and to process post-production waste and by-products from grain processing. The composition and structure of barley grains are shown graphically in Fig. 1 . This approach supports the development of a closed-loop economy. Of particular note are barley bran, hulls and straw, which are cheap and readily available raw materials.

Barley grain has anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, antidiabetic, immunomodulatory, antimicrobial properties and has positive effects on cardiovascular and gastrointestinal function, counteracts obesity and aging [19]. Arabinoxylan contained in the cereal further reduces blood lipids, lowers cholesterol, regulates the body's immune response and improves intestinal peristalsis [20]. C. Yuan et al. demonstrate the potential of using β-glucan extracted from mountain barley to co-microencapsulate with probiotic bacteria and enhances their protective effects during the digestive process in the stomach and intestines [21]. In their study, S. Lee et al. propose the use of β-glucan in cancer therapy. They also point to the possibility of isolating amylase and protease enzymes from hulled barley by carrying out the fermentation process under appropriate conditions [22]. Complementary therapy for pneumonia and dyspnea can be carried out based on barley. The drug based on this grain and its supplementation improves oxygen saturation in the blood, which is extremely important for COVID-19 patients [23]. Barley can also be used on a larger production scale. For example, in brewing, this grain is a highly valued raw material, and the waste generated from processing the grain can be used to produce bioethanol [24]. Barley straw and hulls, due to their content of lignocellulose and its active groups, are suitable for the production of adsorbents for various water pollutants [25]. The starch in the raw material can be used to synthesize a biodegradable biopolymer such as polylactide (PLA) or polyhydroxy acids (PHA) [26]. This biomass can also be a raw material for the production of xylitol and sorbitol, which are used in the food, pharmaceutical and cosmetic industries [27].

Biomass from barley processing can be used in many industries. Due to its high content of organic compounds, it also becomes a good raw material for obtaining carbon-based products. The most common are biocarbon, activated carbon or hydrocarbon. These products are mainly used as sorbents [28–30]. Carbon materials are very popular, and in recent years have also played a significant role in nanotechnology. A modern carbon-based material is a zero-dimensional nanomaterial referred to as Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs). These are quasi-spherical particles <10 nm in size characterized by graphene planes and functional groups on their surface [31,32]. The structure of CQDs affects their unique properties, such as photoluminescence, biocompatibility, low toxicity, good electrical conductivity, chemical and thermal stability, high dispersity [33–35]. This translates into numerous applications for CQDs ranging from medicine, optoelectronics, energy storage, photocatalysis, and electrocatalysis [32,36,37]. The most common use of their photoluminescent properties makes them an excellent tool in bioimaging and molecular diagnostics. Thanks to their ability to bind to biological molecules on their surface, CQDs enable precise identification of cancer cells and molecular biomarkers [38]. Photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT) are gaining popularity in cancer therapy. In PTT, cancer cells are labeled with particles that absorb a specific wavelength of light, leading to the conversion of light energy into heat. This causes a local increase in temperature, denaturation of proteins and death of cancer cells. CQDs show great potential in this method due to their ability to absorb near-infrared (NIR) radiation, which increases the effectiveness of the therapy [39,40]. PDT, on the other hand, uses light-sensitive compounds that generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) and singlet oxygen upon exposure to light, leading to the destruction of cancer cells. To increase the specificity of CQDs, nanocomposites are created with them, such as graphene nanotubes (GNTs), metal nanoparticles, or iron oxides [41]. GNTs are distinguished by their large specific surface area and excellent thermal and electrical conductivity. Their combination with CQDs enhances the efficiency of PDT by improving ROS generation, enhancing biocompatibility, and more precisely targeting cancer cells [42]. Among metal nanoparticle modifications, plasmonic nanoparticles such as gold and silver are of particular interest. Their combination with CQDs enables simultaneous imaging and therapy. CQDs are responsible for precise drug delivery and ROS generation, while plasmonic nanoparticles enhance the photothermal effect through the phenomenon of local resonance of surface plasmons [43]. Another alternative is to use magnetic Fe₃O₄ nanoparticles in combination with CQDs. The synergy of their properties means that the action of CQDs as photosensitizers is further supported by the hyperthermia effect induced by Fe₃O₄. This combination enables more effective destruction of cancer cells, including in the treatment of glioma, while minimizing damage to healthy tissues [44,45].

CQDs also have applications for environmental protection, particularly in the detection and elimination of pollutants. They can act as selective sensors to detect specific substances, and thanks to their easily modifiable surface and high chemical stability, they can be used for adsorption and photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes, pharmaceuticals, and pesticides [46]. The work of R. Muangmor et al. used CQDs obtained from coffee waste to enhance the photocatalytic properties of TiO₂ and ZnO in the removal of pesticides from water [47]. Dyes such as methylene blue and methyl orange can also be efficiently degraded in a similar manner [48,49]. CQDs also perform well in the detection and removal of heavy metal ions and antibiotics from the aquatic environment [50,51]. In addition, their fluorescent properties allow determination of substance concentrations based on light emission measurements. For example, CQDs can be encapsulated in a hydrogel coating, where they enhance the sorbent's ability to bind contaminants due to the presence of functional groups such as -COOH and -OH. W. Abd El-Fattah et al. used such a nanocomposite with CQDs to sorb the herbicide diquat and monitor the process in real time [52]. Another area of research into the application of CQDs is energy conversion, especially in photovoltaic cells, where they can increase their efficiency by broadening the range of absorbed radiation [53]. CQDs are used because of their ability to tune the energy bandwidth, enabling efficient use of low-energy and intensity photons. Due to their high quantum efficiency and excellent photostability, CQDs are used in high-efficiency solar concentrators, improving the power conversion process [54].

This study evaluates the feasibility of using barley bran, including the husk, as a raw material for the synthesis of CQDs using a low-energy, integrated hydrothermal-microwave method. Unlike commonly used lignocellulosic biomass, barley bran has a unique chemical composition, rich in simpler nitrogen-containing organic compounds and polysaccharides, which promotes efficient nitrogen doping, increases the fluorescence intensity of the obtained CQDs and enables synthesis at lower temperatures. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first comprehensive report that not only presents high-emission CQDs obtained from barley bran, but also systematically analyzes the effects of key process parameters on their structural and optical properties. This work makes a new contribution to the field of valorization of agricultural wastes with complex chemical composition and is an important step towards the development of more efficient, fluorescent biomaterials.

The raw material for the synthesis of CQDs was barley bran with hulls (BB) from a flour and barley groats manufacturing and service facility. Sigma-Aldrich's ethylenediamine (EDA, ≥ 99.5 %) was added to the reaction mixture to increase the fluorescence properties of the product. Deionized water was used as a solvent in the reactions carried out. Quinine sulfate monohydrate (QS) (BioReagent, 99.0–101.0 %) from Sigma-Aldrich in H2SO4 medium (96 %) from Roth was used as the fluorescence standard.

The raw material was prepared for synthesis by grinding it in a shear mill and sieving it through a 0.250 mm mesh sieve. The resulting powder was macerated at a ratio of 5 g of raw material per 200 mL of deionized water. The process was carried out for 3 h at 60 °C with continuous stirring at 200 rpm. The macerate was then subjected to several stages of filtration. Initially, gravity filtration was carried out using 260 μm and 125 μm E-D-Schnellsiebe nylon mesh filters. The last step was vacuum filtration on a 1288 grade cellulose filter from Munktell. This yielded a filtrate ready for further synthesis.

CQDs were obtained by a hydrothermal method in a microwave radiation field in Ertec's Magnum II microwave reactor. The syntheses were prepared based on a 3(k-1) fractional plan according to the method of Planning of Experiments (DoE). The independent parameters were process temperature (220; 235; 250 °C), process time (1; 3; 5 h), and filtrate volume (10; 25; 40 mL). The temperature of the process involved setting the appropriate pressure, which for the following temperature values was: 18, 20, 22 bars. A summary of the various synthesis parameters for CQDs samples (C1-C9) is shown in Table 1 . EDA was added to the reaction mixture at a volume ratio of filtrate to EDA of 1:0.021. The sample was replenished with water to 50 mL. After the process, the obtained suspension was vacuum filtered using a 0.1 μm Durapore PVDF (poly(vinylidene fluoride)) hydrophilic filter and dialyzed for 3 h at room temperature with 200 rpm stirring using 3.5 kDa MWCO regenerated cellulose membranes (Spectra/Por 3). The above steps for the preparation of raw material and synthesis of CQDs are shown schematically in Fig. 2 .

The obtained CQDs suspension was subjected to further spectroscopic analysis, including UV–Vis absorbance and fluorescence measurements. All UV–Vis spectra were recorded for 100-fold diluted CQDs suspensions in the 200–800 nm range using quartz cuvettes, with deionized water as the reference. Fluorescence measurements were performed for CQDs at various dilutions (1, 2, 5, 10 and 100-fold) to determine the best concentration for analysis. The 5-fold dilution was chosen because it provided the most reliable fluorescence intensity. The excitation wavelength for CQDs was optimized in the 320–420 nm range, with 380 nm identified as the most effective excitation wavelength, and fluorescence emission spectra were then recorded in the 420–650 nm range. To benchmark fluorescence properties, the CQDs samples were compared to a QS fluorescence standard at a concentration of 10−5 M in 0.5 M H₂SO₄, with characteristic fluorescence parameters of excitation at 347 nm and an emission band spanning 380–580 nm. For direct comparison, fluorescence spectra for QS were measured at excitation 347 nm with emission recorded in the 380–620 nm range. Each fluorescence measurement was repeated three times to ensure repeatability and accuracy. The results obtained were analyzed to evaluate the fluorescence performance of CQDs relative to QS. In developing the results, normalization to a mean standard was used to allow comparison of the fluorescence intensity of the samples against a stable reference standard, thus eliminating the influence of minor experimental variables such as differences in sample preparation or measurement conditions, thus ensuring consistency and reliability of the results in the comparative analysis.

Milled and sieved barley bran was analyzed for dry matter content on a RADWAG MA50/1.R WH halogen weighing machine. The raw material was also characterized by X-ray Diffraction (XRD) with a SmartLab SE powder X-ray diffractometer equipped with a solid-state (2D) X-ray detector Hypix 400 from Rigaku and a high-resolution scanning electron microscope (SEM) Apreo 2 S LoVac (Thermo Fisher Scientific) equipped with X-ray energy dispersive spectrometers - EDS detectors: UltraDry (Thermo Fisher Scientific) and Octane Elect (EDAX Ametek GmbH). Identification of functional groups was carried out by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy with a Thermo Scientific™ Nicolet™ iS™5 FT-IR spectrometer equipped with an iD7 ATR attachment. Elemental analysis of CHNSO (CHN/CHNS EuroEA3000 analyzer, EuroVector) was performed to precipitate from barley bran filtrate subjected to centrifugation at 100,000 rpm for 30 min (Sorvall MX 150, Thermo Scientific).

Suspended CQDs were analyzed by UV–Vis spectroscopy on a Rayleigh UV 1800 (BRAIC) instrument using quartz cuvettes. Particle size and structure were examined by Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) on a Tecnai F20 X-Twin (FEI Europe). Particle size and zeta potential were checked by the Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) method using a Zetasizer NanoZS instrument (Malvern) at material refractive index of 1.86. Zetasizer Software 8.02 was used to calculate and visualize the results. The fluorescence of the suspensions was analyzed on a SpectraMax M2 spectrophotometer (Molecular Devices) on 96-well PS black plates with a transparent flat bottom from VWR. A UV LED lamp with a wavelength of 360 nm was used to photograph the samples in the darkroom. Results were analyzed using OriginPro 2022 9.9.0.225 and Statistica 12 software from StatSoft. The CQDs were also subjected to FT-IR and XRD analysis with prior drying of the suspension to obtain bulk material at 100 °C for 72 h.

To determine the physicochemical properties of BB raw material and its suitability as a precursor for the synthesis of CQDs, a series of analyses were carried out. Measurement of dry weight made it possible to assess the moisture content of the raw material, while XRD, SEM-EDS and FTIR techniques provided information on its crystal structure, surface morphology, elemental composition and the presence of functional groups. The dry weight of the material was measured by measuring the relative humidity of the sample. The average dry matter content of the raw material was 92.93 % ± 0.23 %.

XRD analysis shown in Fig. 3A revealed the amorphous nature of the material. The BB composition is primarily lignocellulosic pulp and starch residues are possible. The presence of a broad and fuzzy peak in the 10–30° range suggests the presence of a complex amorphous phase, consisting of elements with a variety of interatomic distances and local structural ordering. The use of 2θ diffraction angles in the analysis made it possible to distinguish diffraction peaks of 10°, 15°, 18°, 20°, 23°, and 29°. These are peaks characteristic of cellulose, lignin or starch, which show a crystalline-amorphous structure. Cellulose is described by diffraction peaks at about 15°, 17°, 23° [55]. Diffractograms of lignin contain diffraction peaks in the 10–30° range with a characteristic peak at about 20° [56]. Starch, on the other hand, is characterized by diffraction peaks between 10° and 27°. The branched amylopectin contained in it causes the formation of an amorphous structure, while linear amylose increases the degree of crystallinity of starch [57,58]. Comparison of the BB raw material with the CQDs sample (C235-3-25) reveals synthesis-induced structural changes. The C235-3-25 sample shows a more homogeneous amorphous character, devoid of the complex structures observed in the raw material. The broad peak in the 15–25° range still indicates amorphousness but differs in character from that in the BB raw material. In addition, the range of this signal has changed, and its intensity has decreased markedly, which may indicate a simplification of the material's structure as a result of the synthesis process [59,60].

Fig. 3B compares the FT-IR spectrum for the BB raw material and sample dried CQDs (C235-3-25). Analysis of the BB raw material revealed the presence of functional groups associated with hydrocarbons, fats, proteins, and polysaccharides. In the 3200–3600 cm−1 range, a broad band is observed in both samples, but it is more pronounced and sharper in the C235-3-25, suggesting the presence of NH bonds, which may overlap with the characteristic band for -OH group. The crude shows a stronger signal, indicating the loss of OH bonds during the synthesis of CQDs. The NH bonds may come from proteins present in the crude, and the OH from water, lignin, or hemicellulose residues [61]. In the 2800–3000 cm−1 range, the bands are attributed to symmetric and asymmetric vibrations of CH bonds in aliphatic compounds. The sharp signal at 2920 cm−1 is due to the presence of methyl and methylene groups, and the raw material is characterized by higher signal intensity, suggesting a higher content of simple organic compounds such as lignin or fats [62,63]. The band at 2850 cm−1 is attributed to methoxy groups from lignin [63]. The signal at 1715 cm−1, visible only in the BB raw material, comes from carbonyl groups (C=O) in ketones, carbonyls, or esters [64,65]. The 1500–1650 cm−1 range is attributed to NH and CC deformations of aromatic rings [30]. The BB raw material shows intense signals, associated with amide groups (e.g., signal at 1650 cm−1), while the C235-3-25 spectrum is more blurred, indicating the presence of various functional groups, including nitrogen and double bonds of carbons in aromatic system. The signal at 1536 cm−1 may come from amide II (-CO-NH-) groups [61]. In the 1000–1300 cm−1 range, CO, CN vibrations and deformations associated with aromatic rings have been identified. In C235-3-25, signals in this range are less intense and more diverse, suggesting interactions between different functional groups. The signal at 991 cm−1 is attributed to CO, CC, C-C-O vibrations [64].

Elemental analysis was performed on the BB raw material after thermal treatment and filtration to leach out water-soluble compounds, increase their availability, and reduce the lignocellulosic mass content, which improves the efficiency of the process. The raw material has 46.73 ± 0.06 % carbon, 37.20 ± 0.02 % oxygen, 6.69 ± 0.15 % nitrogen, and 9.39 ± 0.10 % hydrogen. The presence of sulfur was not demonstrated. The SEM microphotographs in Fig. 4A-D show the varied surface of the raw BB material. Fragments of fibers, spherical and irregularly shaped structures can be seen. Most of the surface elements do not exceed 100 μm in size. For 10,000-fold magnification, EDS analysis was performed (Fig. 4D), which showed the presence of carbon atoms in 80.2 %, oxygen in 15.8 % and nitrogen in 4.0 % on the surface of the material. There was an even distribution of elements in the studied area.

The synthesized and purified CQDs suspensions were analyzed for their optical properties. The obtained products have a pH of 8–9. The structure of the CQDs was confirmed by UV–Vis and TEM analyses. UV–Vis spectroscopy was used to determine the characteristic absorption bands of the obtained CQDs to obtain information on the electron structure of the particles. TEM was used to directly observe the morphology and determine the size and crystal structure of the nanoparticles. Fig. 5 shows the UV–Vis spectrum of all the obtained CQDs suspensions. Absorbance bands below 300 nm correspond to aromatic π → π* transitions in the CC bond. A distinctive and characteristic peak for CQDs is located at about 280 nm. Broad peaks of lower absorbance in the 280–400 nm range correspond to n → π* transitions in the CO bond [66,67]. The C250-5-40 sample has the highest peak absorbance at around 280 nm at 0.78 a.u. and the C235-1-40 sample 0.71 a.u. The other suspensions show absorbance below 0.50 a.u.

The C235-3-25 sample was subjected to TEM analysis shown in Fig. 6A and B. The microphotographs show an uneven distribution of near-circular structures with clearly visible parallel lines, typical of crystal planes. The observed structures have diameters in the range of 3 to 8 nm, with distances between crystal planes ranging from 0.19 to 0.28 nm. The exact size distribution of the particle diameter is shown in the histogram in Fig. 6C. The largest population consists of particles with diameters of 4 and 5 nm. The average size according to the normal distribution shown is about 5.5 nm. The actual data are centralized around this point, and the distribution approximates a symmetric distribution. For comparison of the obtained data, Fig. 6D shows the size distribution of particle diameter in suspension obtained by the DLS method. This technique makes it possible to determine the average hydrodynamic size and homogeneity of the dispersion, which is important for further applications in solution. In this case, the distribution is also not monomodal and the particle size is clustered between 10 and 100 nm.

Fluorescence analysis was carried out to evaluate the light emission properties of CQDs. The dependence of the emission intensity on the excitation wavelength was studied, and the properties of the obtained CQDs were compared with the commercial fluorescent standard QS, which allows the evaluation of the photoluminescence efficiency of the obtained material. The fluorescence of CQDs suspensions was studied using 5-fold dilutions to minimize the emission suppression effects observed in more concentrated solutions. The results were compared with a 10−5 M QS standard solution, whose absorbance at 347 nm is 0.02. Fig. 7A shows the emission spectra of the substance upon excitation at 347 nm. To allow comparison with the fluorescence standard, a dotted line representing QS emission has been added to the graph. The maxima of the emission peaks of the samples studied are in the range from 440 to 450 nm. Of the samples analyzed, sample C250-5-40 shows the highest fluorescence relative to the QS standard. Samples C250-1-25 and C235-1-40 have emission comparable to QS. The samples mentioned also had the highest absorbance. The decision to choose excitation at 347 nm was based on the properties of the fluorescence standard used. Nevertheless, the most favorable excitation for the obtained CQDs suspensions was 380 nm, as presented in Fig. 7B. At this excitation also the C235-1-40, C250-1-25, and C250-5-40 samples show the highest fluorescence with intensities between 8500 and 9500 RFU. Satisfactory results were also obtained for sample C220-5-25, whose intensity was around 8000 RFU. Samples C220-3-40 and C235-3-25 reached fluorescence values of around 7000 RFU. The lowest emission intensity was recorded for samples C220-1-10, C235-5-10, and C250-3-10, whose spectra did not exceed 2500 RFU. For all samples tested, the maximum of the emission peak at 380 nm excitation was at 464 nm. The fluorescence emission of the obtained CQDs is characterized by a wide range, with peaks extending to about 200 nm. Fig. 7C shows images of a reference sample (water) and a sample of CQDs (C250-5-40) under daylight, in which the fluorescent suspension takes on a yellow-orange color. Illumination of the C250-5-40 sample with a UV lamp with a wavelength of 360 nm results in cyan-blue emission, according to the results shown in Fig. 7B.

More precise results of radiation emission peaks for samples at 380 nm excitation are given in Table 2 . The value of the peak maximum increases proportionally to the area under the peak. The standard deviations for most samples do not exceed 2 %, indicating good repeatability of the results.

To determine the influence of the synthesis parameters on the structural and optical properties of the obtained CQDs, statistical analysis was carried out. It allowed the identification of the most significant factors determining the quality of the final product and the selection of the best parameters of the synthesis process. The fractional plan, based on which the syntheses were carried out, was analyzed for the effect of process variables on the results obtained. Pareto analysis showed that with a statistical significance level of p = 0.05, in the case of absorbance at 280 nm, the volume of the filtrate and the process temperature have the greatest influence, with both variables showing a linear relationship. For fluorescence peak magnitude at 380 nm excitation, filtrate volume is an important factor, showing both a linear and quadratic effect. Other process variables have insignificant effect on the resulting fluorescence of suspensions.

Considering the results of the Pareto analysis, profiles of predicted values and desirability were generated to determine the optimal conditions for synthesizing CQDs with the given properties. The most desirable results include: the highest possible absorbance at 280 nm, and the highest possible fluorescence at 380 nm excitation. Fig. 8 shows profile analyzing different combinations of process parameters and examines the effect of variables on the absorbance and fluorescence of the samples. Optimal conditions indicate the need for a high process temperature and a large filtrate volume with a short process time to achieve the desired results. In this analysis, a maximum desirability rate of about 99.87 % was achieved.

The result of using a higher process temperature is the use of higher pressure, which affects reaction dynamics and process control. Higher temperature accelerates the kinetics of chemical reactions, which leads to more efficient decomposition of compounds in the reaction mixture and the formation of quantum dots with the desired properties. Pressure and temperature are also related to the volume of the filtrate. Too low a pressure with a high concentration of the reaction mixture can reduce the efficiency of the process, resulting in fewer fluorescent CQDs. On the other hand, higher pressure aids in controlling the process, limiting the growth of the forming structures too quickly, resulting in quantum dots of better quality. In the syntheses carried out, higher temperature, higher filtrate volume and the associated higher pressure caused an increase in the absorbance and fluorescence intensity of the obtained CQDs suspensions. The obtained results indicate that the use of higher values of process parameters allows to obtain more quantum dots capable of emitting radiation with higher fluorescence, which makes them more optically efficient.

In the conducted research, it should be noted that barley bran, which is, among other things, an agricultural waste, was used for the synthesis of CQDs. Most often, lignocellulosic wastes such as wheat straw [68,69], rice husks [70], fruit pomace [71,72] or wood biomass [73,74] are used for the synthesis of various carbon materials. There is a lack of publications on CQDs obtained from barley bran. They are distinguished by their specific chemical composition and make them an interesting raw material and carbon source.

BB raw material is characterized by a high carbon content, reaching up to 80 % after surface milling. The presence of lignocellulosic pulp mainly provides a high carbon content. The raw material is rich in compounds from various chemical groups, such as polysaccharides, proteins, fatty acids, and triglycerides, resulting in the significant presence of oxygen atoms. Nitrogen, conversely, comes mainly from amide and amine groups present in proteins. The raw material is subjected to thermal treatment and separation of larger fractions to increase the availability of water-soluble components. The hydrothermal-microwave synthesis method adopted is low-energy, and due to the maximum process temperature of 250 °C, the decomposition of cellulose, lignin, and hemicellulose will not be efficient. For this reason, most of the lignocellulose mass is separated from the suspension. Despite these treatments, the material prepared for CQDs synthesis contains a high total carbon content of about 47 %.

The organic compounds in the BB filter used for synthesis are carbon precursors. They undergo dehydration, decarboxylation and aromatization processes, leading to condensation and formation of carbon structures [75]. The electronic and optical properties of CQDs can be modified by changing their structure, such as by doping with metals. Ethylenediamine plays a role in the synthesis of the nitrogen source, which is incorporated into the resulting structure. The synthesis was performed under hydrothermal conditions at temperatures of 220, 235, and 250 °C, pressures of 18, 20, and 22 bar, and reaction times of 1, 3, and 5 h. The application of microwave radiation ensured rapid and uniform heating of the entire mixture, promoting consistent reaction progress. This facilitated the formation of uniform, well-dispersed dots. Under these conditions, thermal degradation of the compounds contained in the filter occurs. The processes of decarboxylation, dehydration and aromatization occur sequentially, resulting in the condensation of small organic molecules into the first outlines of carbon nuclei. Once a critical degree of condensation is reached, the process of nucleation, or the formation of small, stable carbon nuclei, begins. Subsequent fragments combine with the already formed nuclei, leading to the growth of particles to the size characteristic of quantum dots [76,77]. During growth, nitrogen atoms provided by EDA are incorporated into the carbon network. They can take the form of amine groups or be an integral part of the aromatic structure, which alters the electron and optical structure of the dots [78].

The study confirms that the chosen synthesis method can be used to obtain CQDs from barley bran. Process parameters such as temperature, synthesis time and the volume of the filtrate after raw material treatment have a key effect on the properties of the final product. Analysis of XRD and FT-IR spectra reveals significant differences between the raw material and the CQDs synthesized during the process. The diffractogram of the CQD sample shows structural changes induced by thermal treatment, characterized by simplification and homogenization of the amorphous structure of the original raw material. However, diffraction analysis alone does not provide conclusive evidence of CQD formation. Confirmation of CQD synthesis required additional analyses, such as optical property measurements and morphological characterization, which offer more definitive evidence of carbon nanostructures. This perspective is supported by the findings of A. Pal et al. who in their review highlight the formation of amorphous CQDs as a function of selected synthesis methods and parameters [60]. Analysis of FT-IR spectra indicates the loss of intense OH and CH signals for CQDs and the appearance of jagged signals in the 1500–1650 cm−1 range and differentiated bands in the 1000–1300 cm−1 range. Such changes suggest the introduction of nitrogen groups and the formation of more complex structures with double bonds and aromatic rings. The doping of CQDs with nitrogen allows the formation of different forms of nitrogen bonded to carbon atoms, which has a direct impact on the resulting structure. The work of J. Gan and co-authors points to potential nitrogen‑carbon configurations that differ in the manner of hybridization interactions. For example, these could be sp2-hybridized groups, such as CNC, CNC porphyrin structures, as well as amine N groups and quaternary N groups. Surface attachment of nitrogen to the CQDs structure leads to the formation of various nitrogen surface states, which play a key role in shaping the optical properties of the product [79].

As a result of the UV–Vis analysis, it was concluded that the higher peak absorbance at around 280 nm, characteristic of the CC bond, is correlated with higher particle fluorescence. This is consistent with the results of previous studies, which indicate that the presence of aromatic systems and π-π* bonds in carbon structures favors their optical properties. In addition, the larger volume of the filtrate increases the availability of precursors in solution, which may result in more homogeneous particle formation. The best fluorescence results were obtained for C235-1-40, C250-1-25, and C250-5-40 samples, which were synthesized at 250 °C or 235 °C, for different times, using larger volumes of filtrate. In the C235-3-25 sample, particle size and structure were examined using TEM analysis. The sizes of the obtained crystalline particles and the distance between the crystalline shells duplicate the results in numerous studies, which were compiled, among others, by the teams of J. Gan et al. [79], C. Das et al. [80], and A. Kundu et al. [81]. For comparison purposes, the results were compared with the particle size distribution analysis conducted by DLS. The C235-3-25 sample was obtained at 235 °C, with a synthesis time of 3 h and a filtrate volume of 25 mL. TEM analysis revealed a particle distribution in the range of 3–8 nm, with a dominant population of 4 nm in size. In contrast, the DLS method showed an average particle size of 32.0 ± 6.2 nm in suspension, with a diameter distribution in the range of 10–100 nm. The discrepancy in results between TEM and DLS is due to differences in measurement mechanisms. TEM allows visualization of individual particles, while DLS measures the hydrodynamic distribution of particles in a suspension, which also takes aggregates into account. The presence of larger structures in the suspension, confirmed by DLS and darker areas on TEM microphotographs, suggests a tendency for particles to aggregate in aqueous environments. Aggregation is a typical phenomenon for colloidal carbon dots, especially under aqueous conditions, where van der Waals interactions and electrostatic forces can promote the formation of larger structures. In a study by Z. Wan et al. they show that aggregation may also be desirable for CQDs to obtain better fluorescence. In some cases, the structures of CQDs may give worse results when they are dispersed. Under the influence of aggregation and structure enhancement, there is a reduction in vibration, bond rotation, and nonradiative decomposition manifested by a significant increase in the luminescence of the suspension. This phenomenon is referred to as restriction of the intramolecular motion (RIM) [82].

Nevertheless, microscopic analysis confirmed the presence of crystalline carbon structures obtained from the BB raw material. Statistical analysis showed that the optimal choice of process parameters, such as temperature of 250 °C, time of 1 h and 40 mL of filtrate, allows obtaining CQDs with almost 100 % certainty, characterized by a high absorbance peak at 280 nm and intense fluorescence at 380 nm excitation with an emission peak at 464 nm. The obtained CQDs show broad emission of radiation, with a peak maximum at 464 nm in the blue light range.

Among the compared materials shown in Table 3 , CQDs synthesized from waste biomass such as potato starch, rice husks, and lignin also displayed emissions in the blue region, and were proposed for applications including fluorescent sensing of metal ions, biological imaging, and detection of biologically relevant analytes such as vitamin C and Pb2+. The synthesis of CQDs from pure substances such as glucose or citric acid yields particles with similar optical properties to those obtained from biomass. This demonstrates that biomass can serve as a viable feedstock for CQD production. However, a notable drawback is the greater difficulty in achieving a homogeneous particle structure. Therefore, synthesis methods, parameter optimization, and effective purification processes are critical to overcoming this challenge. Barley bran CQDs with focused emission at 464 nm make them suitable for in vitro imaging of superficial tissue layers and biological structures, despite limited in vivo penetration due to their emission range. Importantly, these CQDs exhibit significant potential as biomolecular sensors, capable of detecting a broad range of compounds commonly present in blood and urine, such as enzymes, glucose, uric acid, cortisol, metal ions, proteins, urea, and toxins through fluorescence quenching or enhancement mechanisms. In addition, they can find application in the detection of cancer markers and compounds from viruses [94]. Thus, the synthesized CQDs from barley bran not only align with the performance and applications of CQDs obtained from other biomass sources, but also offer a highly effective, reproducible, and sustainable alternative for use in fluorescent biosensing and diagnostic platforms.

This study demonstrates that barley bran, an agricultural waste, can be effectively used as a precursor for synthesizing CQDs, promoting both economic sustainability and a circular economy approach. Compared to other agricultural residues, barley bran's unique chemical composition makes it a promising carbon source. The hydrothermal-microwave synthesis employed here proved to be energy-efficient, but the tested temperatures did not fully degrade cellulose, lignin, and hemicellulose. However, increasing the processing temperature enhances the availability of water-soluble components, which is crucial for achieving the desired fluorescence properties and small particle size. Structural analyses (XRD and FT-IR) revealed significant simplification and homogenization of the amorphous structure, with notable changes in -OH and -CH groups and the introduction of nitrogen functionalities, leading to the formation of aromatic and conjugated structures. The optimized synthesis conditions (250 °C, 1 h, 40 mL of filtrate) ensure nearly 100 % certainty of obtaining high absorbance at 280 nm. Additionally, controlling suspension volume enables the formation of particles smaller than 10 nm, with interplanar spacings of 0.19–0.28 nm.

The results presented in this article highlight the effectiveness of the hydrothermal-microwave method in synthesizing CQDs from barley bran with desirable optical properties. Further research should focus on optimizing the stability of CQDs suspensions and using surface functionalization to reduce aggregation and improve fluorescence properties in different environments. Great emphasis should also be placed on proper preparation of the raw material and effective purification of the product. These activities can improve the fluorescent properties of CQDs and allow their use as fluorescent sensors in biomedicine or environmental protection.
